text: Based on the amount of food components in 100 kcal, 100g or 100ml of food as the Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC) or 50g of food, the amount of certain food components in some foods cannot be obtained reasonably. Then, the foods that can be reasonably selected for reducing the risk of chronic diseases are discussed. By implementing the method of calculating the food components, determining the suitable levels of food components and evaluating the nutritional quality according to the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines, 8,596 food samples in 25 food categories and 29 food components were evaluated. According to the FDA or CAC guidelines, the food components selected from some reasonable foods met the requirements of positive nutrients for sufficient intake, while the CAC guideline did not meet the requirements of positive nutrients. Conversely, the food components selected from some reasonable foods that met the requirements of positive nutrients did not meet the criteria of CAC guideline for negative nutrients such as cholesterol, energy, fat, saturated fat and sodium. Moreover, the food components selected from some unreasonable foods did not meet the criteria of CAC guideline for negative nutrients. Finally, the basis for selecting foods based on RACC has been proved to be inferior for being fast foods based on FDA/CAC serving size (obtained from RACC), as well as spices and herbs based on the CAC in 100g or 100ml and as for vegetables and vegetable products based on the CAC in 100kcal. The former three types of food categories are found to have the highest average scores of positive nutrients for nutritional quality of food among children ≥ 4 years and adults.
explanation: The text is coherent and domain-specific, with plausible data and references that an expert could produce. Some awkward phrasing and repetition suggest it could be AI-generated or AI-assisted, though it could also be a human-written abstract.
